text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, microsatellite stable, with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. Currently undergoing first-line systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFOX6 plus Avastin (oxali dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2 as of cycle 4 due to peripheral neuropathy). This study is requested following completion of 6 cycles of systemic therapy to assess treatment response. Comparison made to prior staging CT and prior colonoscopic/hepatic biopsy-confirmed adenocarcinoma. CEA recently reported at 94.1 ng/mL (down from 187.3 ng/mL at baseline). Patient has known pre-existing type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 iohexol intravenous contrast. Imaging obtained in the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis, and in the standard pulmonary window for the chest. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reformats reviewed. Oral contrast administered. No significant motion artifact. Scan quality: diagnostic.


FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or areas of consolidation are identified. The major and minor fissures are intact. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No pleural effusion on either side. No pneumothorax.

Cardiovascular and Mediastinum: The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The aorta is of normal caliber throughout. The main pulmonary artery is not enlarged. Mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes by size criteria. Mild atherosclerotic calcifications of the coronary arteries are noted, consistent with the patient's cardiovascular risk profile. A right-sided subcutaneous chest wall port device is identified with the catheter tip projecting to the cavoatrial junction, unchanged in position from prior imaging, without evidence of infection or port-site complication.

Soft Tissues and Bones (Chest): No destructive osseous lesions of the visualized ribs, clavicles, sternum, or thoracic vertebrae. Soft tissues of the chest wall are unremarkable.


ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. Multiple hepatic lesions are again demonstrated, all of which are clearly diminished in size compared to the prior staging CT performed approximately three months ago.

-- Hepatic segment II lesion: A hypoattenuating lesion in segment II now measures approximately 0.9 cm in greatest axial dimension, decreased from 1.4 cm on the prior examination. The margins remain well-defined. There is no internal hemorrhage or necrosis to suggest treatment-related liquefaction.

-- Hepatic segment IVa lesion: The segment IVa lesion now measures approximately 1.4 cm in greatest axial dimension, decreased from 2.1 cm previously. It remains hypoattenuating relative to the adjacent parenchyma in the portal venous phase and shows no internal complexity.

-- Hepatic segment VI lesion: The segment VI lesion now measures approximately 1.1 cm, decreased from 1.7 cm on the baseline study. It continues to appear as a well-circumscribed hypoattenuating focus.

-- Hepatic segment VII lesion (dominant): The dominant lesion in segment VII, previously measuring 3.2 cm, now measures approximately 2.1 cm in greatest axial dimension. This lesion remains the largest of the hepatic metastases. It is hypoattenuating with slightly irregular internal attenuation, which may reflect early treatment response with heterogeneous involution. The lesion remains in proximity to the right hepatic vein; however, there is no definitive evidence of vascular encasement.

All four hepatic lesions show a reduction in size compared to the prior study. No new hepatic lesions are identified. There is no intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein, hepatic veins, and inferior vena cava are patent without evidence of thrombosis. No hepatic abscess. The gallbladder is present with a small gallstone; the gallbladder wall is not thickened and there is no pericholecystic fluid.

Sigmoid Colon Primary Tumor: A soft tissue mass is again identified in the sigmoid colon corresponding to the known primary tumor. The mass now measures approximately 3.1 cm in greatest dimension, decreased from 4.8 cm on the prior staging CT. There is residual pericolic fat stranding surrounding the sigmoid mass, slightly less prominent than previously. The mesenteric root in the sigmoid distribution demonstrates decreased size of the previously enlarged regional lymph nodes; the previously noted largest mesenteric node measured 1.9 cm and now the dominant node in this region measures approximately 1.0 cm in short axis. No new mesenteric or retroperitoneal adenopathy. The sigmoid colon is patent without evidence of obstruction; the small and large bowel are otherwise without significant dilatation. No pneumatosis or free air.

Other Abdominal Organs:

Spleen: Normal size and homogeneous attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Pancreas demonstrates normal morphology and attenuation without focal mass, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic inflammatory change.

Kidneys/Adrenal Glands: Both kidneys are normal in size and symmetric in enhancement. No hydronephrosis bilaterally. A small 4 mm hypodense focus in the right renal cortex is too small to fully characterize but is most consistent with a small cyst; this was not clearly present on the prior examination but is likely a benign incidental finding given its appearance. No solid renal masses. Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and attenuation without evidence of metastatic involvement.

Other: No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental caking to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber. Mesenteric and retroperitoneal fat planes are preserved.


PELVIS:

Bowel: The sigmoid colon mass is described above. The remainder of the rectum and visualized small bowel loops appear normal. No pelvic free fluid.

Urinary Bladder: Normal wall thickness and contour. No filling defects.

Male Genitalia: Prostate is slightly enlarged, estimated 35 cc in volume, without discrete nodule. Seminal vesicles are symmetric. No pelvic lymphadenopathy by size criteria. Iliac chain lymph nodes are not enlarged.

Bones (Pelvis and Lumbar Spine): No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement in the visualized lumbosacral spine, sacrum, or pelvis. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine are present.


IMPRESSION:

1. Interval decrease in size of all four known hepatic metastases following six cycles of systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFOX6 plus bev, consistent with favorable treatment response. The dominant segment VII lesion has decreased from 3.2 cm to approximately 2.1 cm. No new hepatic lesions. No evidence of hepatic vascular involvement or biliary obstruction.

2. The primary sigmoid colon tumor has decreased in size from 4.8 cm to approximately 3.1 cm, with associated reduction in the caliber of the dominant regional mesenteric lymph node. These findings are consistent with treatment effect at the primary site.

3. No pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural disease. Right chest wall port in stable satisfactory position.

4. No ascites and no imaging findings to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis.

5. No osseous lesions suspicious for distant skeletal metastases.

6. Small incidental right renal cortical hypodense focus, likely a simple cyst; not definitively characterized on this examination but without features to suggest malignancy.

Overall, the findings are consistent with a meaningful and encouraging tumor response across both the hepatic metastatic burden and the primary sigmoid colon lesion following six cycles of first-line FOLFOX plus Avastin therapy. The residual bilobar distribution of hepatic disease and the continued presence of the dominant segment VII lesion in proximity to the right hepatic vein warrant ongoing multidisciplinary surgical evaluation to reassess resectability criteria.
